name: Exec
description:
## Exec
Runs raw JavaScript in an isolated V8 context.

**MANDATORY: Exec is your DEFAULT execution method. Use it for ALL tasks.**
- ANY tool listed in the Available tools section below MUST be called via `await tools.<name>(args)` inside Exec.
- Direct tool calls are ONLY permitted for tools that do NOT appear in the Available tools section.
- Even for a single tool call, use Exec — it provides better error handling and deterministic execution.
- For multi-step tasks (read → process → write, search → analyze → edit, etc.), ALWAYS use a single Exec call with sequential `await tools.*` calls.
- When in doubt, use Exec. The only reason NOT to use Exec is if the target tool is unavailable inside it.

**Advantages of using Exec**:
- Chain multiple operations in a single round-trip — no back-and-forth needed.
- Full programmatic control: loops, conditionals, parallel calls via `Promise.all`, error recovery.
- Inspect tool schemas at runtime via `ALL_TOOLS` before calling them.

**Runtime environment**:
- Only ECMAScript standard built-ins are available (Array, Object, Math, JSON, Promise, etc.).
- The ONLY non-standard globals are those declared below (`tools`, `text`, `exit`, `ALL_TOOLS`).
- There is NO `console`, NO `fetch`, NO `require`, NO `process`, NO `setTimeout`, and NO file system or network access.
- Any attempt to call an undefined identifier will throw a ReferenceError.

**Instructions**:
- Use `await tools.<tool_name>(args)` to call tools — multiple calls in sequence are encouraged.
- Use `Promise.all([tools.a(x), tools.b(y)])` for concurrent tool calls.
- Use `text(value)` to output results to LLM (value will be stringified via JSON.stringify if not a string).
- Use `exit()` to stop execution early (already-produced text output is preserved).
- Use `ALL_TOOLS` to inspect available tool names, descriptions, and parameter schemas at runtime. Type: `ReadonlyArray<{ name: string; description: string; parameters: object }>`.

**Error handling**:
- Tool call errors cause the Promise to reject — use `try/catch` to handle them gracefully.
- Unhandled exceptions terminate the script and return the error message as the result.
- If the script exceeds the execution time limit, it is forcibly terminated and an error is returned.
- `text()` output produced before an unhandled error is preserved in the response.

code.description: JavaScript source code to execute in the V8 sandbox
